coredata.dc:title: Green fabrication of biomass-derived carbon dots and bio-based coatings: Potential of enhancing postharvest quality on Chinese flowering cabbage

coredata.dc:description: The objective of this study was to develop a bio-nanocomposite coating (CQSC) by combining chitosan quaternary ammonium salt (CQAS) and sericin (SC) with biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) to extend the shelf life of Chinese flowering cabbage (CFC). The effects of different concentrations of CDs (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1.0 mg/mL) on the physicochemical, structural, and functional activity of nanocomposite particles were evaluated. CQAS exhibited strong inhibitory effects against Escherichia coli and Bacillus subtilis. Moreover, the application of CQSC on CFC significantly reduced mass losses, slowed the increase in lignin content, maintained ascorbic acid and chlorophyll levels, inhibited the growth of microorganisms, and preserved the unique texture and aroma of CFC during storage at 10 °C compared with uncoated CFC. The results will contribute to the further development of CDs coatings to improve the postharvest preservation effect of fruits and vegetables.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The objective of this study was to develop a bio-nanocomposite coating (CQSC) by combining chitosan quaternary ammonium salt (CQAS) and sericin (SC) with biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) to extend the shelf life of Chinese flowering cabbage (CFC). The effects of different concentrations of CDs (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1.0 mg/mL) on the physicochemical, structural, and functional activity of nanocomposite particles were evaluated. CQAS exhibited strong inhibitory effects against Escherichia coli and Bacillus subtilis. Moreover, the application of CQSC on CFC significantly reduced mass losses, slowed the increase in lignin content, maintained ascorbic acid and chlorophyll levels, inhibited the growth of microorganisms, and preserved the unique texture and aroma of CFC during storage at 10 °C compared with uncoated CFC. The results will contribute to the further development of CDs coatings to improve the postharvest preservation effect of fruits and vegetables.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The postharvest fruits and vegetables' quick senescence, dehydration, growth of harmful microorganisms, and other quality decline that leads to significant food waste (Riseh, Vatankhah, Hassanisaadi, & Kennedy, 2023). As a practical solution, functional coatings can be formed into semi-permeable films that protect against mechanical damage, gas exchange, and microbial infection (Riseh, Vatankhah, Hassanisaadi, Shafiei-Hematabad, & Kennedy, 2024). Currently, active food packaging based on bio-based coating is currently widely used to preserve postharvest fruit because of its biocompatibility and environmentally friendly (Al-Tayyar, Youssef, & Al-hindi, 2020; Jafarzadeh, Nafchi, Salehabadi, Oladzad-abbasabadi, & Jafari, 2021). Coatings like this have largely supplanted the use of harmful synthetic fungicides and traditional synthetic waxes (Rangaraj, Rambabu, Banat, & Mittal, 2021; Sivakumar, Gunes, & Romanazzi, 2021).

One eco-friendly option is bio-based coatings made from natural polymers and other biodegradable ingredients (Dai et al., 2024; Mohamed, El-Sakhawy, & El-Sakhawy, 2020; Song et al., 2024). The biodegradability, antimicrobial, and antioxidant characteristics of sericin, a substance derived from silkworms, make it an attractive candidate (Seo, Das, Shin, & Patra, 2023), previous studies have demonstrated via rats that the potential use of sericin as a functional food ingredient (Qin et al., 2020). Recycling sericin from silk processing effluent efficiently improves sustainability (Li, Shi, Wang, & Zhu, 2015), and merging polymers, including sericin with chitosan quaternary ammonium salt (CQAS), improves coating performance by addressing mechanical and water barrier restrictions (Min et al., 2020; Qiu et al., 2023).

Efficacy is limited by their difficult extraction method and single functionality, although research is focused on integrating bio-based coatings with natural antioxidants (such as phytosterols, flavonoids, Plant essential oils) (Ban et al., 2023; Song et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2023). Due to their microscopic size and origin in food precursors, carbon dots (CDs) provide antibacterial characteristics and high yields (Fang et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024). Research has shown that using CDs in food packaging minimizes the loss of nutritional content and increases food shelf life (Fu et al., 2022). Moreover, CDs produced using real food as a carbon source exhibit good edible-qualities when used as fillers in bio-based coatings, together with outstanding biocompatibility and low biotoxicity (Meng et al., 2019; Zhao, Zhang, Wang, & Devahastin, 2020).

In this study, biologically active CDs were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method using banana as a precursor. Subsequently, the synthesized CDs were doped into CQAS-SC (CQS) matrix as nanofillers with different ratios to obtain a series of biologically active and controllable functional nanocomposite particles. The optical, and structural properties of the nanocomposite particles after the addition of CDs were investigated. In addition, the antimicrobial properties against Escherichia coli (E. coil) and Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) were obtained based on the evaluation of the inhibition zone. Finally, the performance of CQAS-SC-CDs (CQSC) composite coating for practical applications on Chinese flowering cabbage in 10 °C environments and their effects on appearance, nutritional and texture were investigated.

Chitosan quaternary ammonium salt was purchased from Macklin Co., Ltd., (Shanghai, China) with a deacetylation degree >95 % and viscosity of 100–200 mPa.s. Sericin was purchased from Peptide Son Biotechnology (Xi ‘an) Co., Ltd. Bananas were purchased from a local supermarket. All chemical agents were analytical grade and used without further purification unless otherwise stated. Bacterial strains were obtained from the laboratory C2–413 in School of Biological and Chemical Engineering, ZUST.

Utilizing banana peel as the precursor, CDs were hydrothermally manufactured using following approach. In summary, 20 g of banana peel paste were added to a reactor holding 100 mL of ultrapure water, which was then heated to 180 °C for 8 h. After being collected, the supernatant was filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane. After that, the CDs were freeze-dried to produce a powder after being dialyzed for 48 h with a 500 Da molecular weight limit.

The particle size of the CDs was assessed using Master Sizers at a temperature of 25 °C. The UV spectrophotometer-2600 was used to get the UV–vis absorption spectra of the aqueous solution containing carbon nanoparticles. The FL970 fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to quantify the reduction in fluorescence of CDs using a quartz cell with a thickness of 1 mm. The morphology of the CDs was analyzed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on a Tecnai F30 electron microscopy equipment operating at a voltage of 300 kV. Raman spectra were collected on a Raman microscope (Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution, Japan) with a laser excitation at 532 nm. The X-ray analysis was performed in sample of CDs using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) at 40 kV and 40 mA. In continuous mode, the data were taken in 10° steps for 1 min at a time over an angle range of 10° to 80° (2θ). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) with a range of 4000 to 500 cm−1 was used to record the samples.

The CQS solution was prepared by dissolving 3 g of CQAS powder and 1 g of SC powder in 100 mL of distilled water. The mixture was stirred continuously for approximately 5 h until the solution became transparent. A variety of CDs with different qualities were dissolved in 100 mL of CQS solution at 25 °C and 300 Hz ultrasounded for 40 min. The composite coating that was obtained was designated as CQSC nanocomposite particles at concentrations of 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1.0 mg/mL.

Protein intrinsic fluorescence spectroscopy was used to find out how SC and CDs interacted with each other. To make the measurement solution, the coating solution was diluted 100 times with PBS solution. A 305 nm excitation wavelength was used for spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-4500, Tokyo, Japan), and release fluorescence was seen between 350 and 600 nm. The voltage was set to 520 V, and the excitation and emission slit sizes were both set to 5 nm. Three identical tests were done for each subject.

The particle size of the SC, CQAS and CQSC (0.2 mg/mL) were assayed by a Master Sizers (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) at 25 °C. Each value is the average of three measurements. Before each test, all samples were diluted with 0.01 mol/L PBS to eliminate the impact of multiple light-scattering.

The morphology of CQSC nanocomposite particles was observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (HT-7800, Japan) with a voltage of 80 kV. Essentially, 230-mesh copper grids were coated with a single drop of diluted emulsion, which was then negatively stained with 1 % (w/v) phosphotungstic acid. Lastly, the sample was left to dry overnight in a ventilated atmosphere.

The crystalline characteristics of particles in SC, CQAS, CDs and CQSC powder was evaluated using X-ray diffraction (H-12, Japan). In continuous mode, the data were taken in 10° steps for 1 min at a time over an angle range of 10° to 80° (2θ).

The FT-IR spectra of the CQAS, SC, CDs, and CQSC were obtained using an FT-IR analyzer (BRUKER V70, Bruker Inc., Germany) via KBr in transmittance mode. The spectra were recorded with a scanning range of 4000–500 cm−1, a scanning rate of 32 scans, and a step size of 4 cm−1 at 25 °C. Each sample was thoroughly dried by freeze-drying it beforehand.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The antibacterial properties of nanocomposite particles
ce:section.ce:para: The capacity of the CQSC nanocomposite particles to inhibit B. subtilis and E. coli was verified using the Oxford cup method (Ban et al., 2023). The control group in this study was the CQS. The concentration of CDs was taken as 0.2 mg/mL, 0.6 mg/mL, 1.0 mg/mL to prepare the composite system. PCA medium was supplemented with suspensions of B. subtilis and E. coli spores, each containing 105 CFU/mL. B. subtilis and E. coli was cultivated for 24 h at 37 °C. The average inhibition zone (mm in diameter), which was measured during the triplicate testing, was used to determine the antibacterial activity of the CQSC nanocomposite particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in fresh-keeping of Chinese flowering cabbage

ce:section.ce:para: Chinese flowering cabbage (Brassica campestris L. ssp. chinensis var. utilis Tsen et Lee, CFC) used in this study was obtained from a local commercial farm in Hangzhou, Zhejiang Province, China. The harvested CFC were transported to the laboratory within 2 h of harvesting. The selection process involved choosing CFC with uniformity in size, maturity, and without any mechanical damage or visible defects. To prepare the CFC for the experiment, they were gently washed and dried. Then, the CFC were randomly divided into four groups, each with three biological replicates. Each group was subjected to a different spraying treatment. The four spraying options investigated were as follows: (1) sterile water, (2) CQS solution, (3, 4) CQSC-0.2 and − 1.0 solution (the concentration of CDs 0.2 mg/mL and 1.0 mg/mL in CQSC). 5 mL of the respective treatment solution were evenly sprayed onto the leaves of the CFC. After the spraying treatment, the samples were placed upright in baskets with holes to allow for drying and storage. All of the CFC samples were treated at 25 °C, 60 % RH and stored at 10 °C, 60 % RH (ambient condition). They were then kept in ambient circumstances, which simulated temperature abuse and were ideal for applying water barrier coatings. These conditions were also suitable for the application of water barrier coatings. For biochemical analyses, the second layer and third layer leaves of the CFC (from the outside-in) were selected. The blade edge and middle main vein were removed from the selected leaves. Samples were collected at 0, 2, 4, 6, and 8 d after the treatment, and were immediately used for the biochemical analyses (Yu et al., 2022).

Weight loss was measured using gravimetric method. Based on the starting mass (W0) and the mass at two-day intervals (Wi), Chinese flowering cabbage samples were weighed. Weight loss was calculated as:

The samples (1 g) were combined with 10 mL of 80 % acetone, and then centrifuged for 15 min at 8000 rpm. Utilizing a UV spectrophotometer, the absorbance of the supernatant at 645 nm (A645) and 663 nm (A663) was measured. The amount of chlorophyll was calculated:

A modified version of Su's approach was used to undertake the sensory evaluation of Chinese flowering cabbage (Su et al., 2022). Ten assessors who had received training in quality control procedures assessed the color, texture, odor, and overall appearance of the lettuce samples that had undergone various treatments. A sensory assessment scale with a range of 0 to 5 was used: Excellent/crisp: 5, moderate crispness: 3, poor/limp: 1; Texture: 5, excellent/crisp: 5, moderate crispness: 3, poor/dim: 1; Odor: 5, no distinct aroma: 3, poor/slight off-odor: 1; Overall visual quality (OVQ): 5, moderate: 3, poor/no fresh appearance: 1. The mean values were computed for each sample's sensory scores. The panelists were randomly assigned to receive the CFC samples, and they each performed their own independent sensory analyses.

A 2,6-dichloroindophenol test was used to determine the ascorbic acid concentration (Lei et al., 2022). Oxalic acid (2 %, 10 mL) and the samples (10 g) were combined to create a volume of 100 mL. After that, the mixture was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min. Titrating the supernatant against 2,6-dichloroindophenol was done. The content of ascorbic acid was computed as:
Ascorbic acid

where V is the total volume of CFC extract in milliliters (mL); V

0 is the volume of dyestuff consumed during the control titration in milliliters (mL); ρ is the mass of one milliliter of dyestuff equivalent to vitamin C (mg/mL); Vs is the volume of sample extract taken during the titration in milliliters (mL); m is the mass of CFC (g).

According to Huang's description, lignin analysis was measured (Huang et al., 2013). The root components were extracted using 95 % ethanol, and the resulting homogenates were centrifuged for 20 min at 5000 rpm. Three to four washes each with 95 % ethanol were performed on the precipitates. For 30 min at 80 °C, the naturally dried precipitates were dissolved in acetyl bromide in glacial acetic acid (25 %, V/V). 2 M NaOH was used to halt the reaction, and 1 mL of cold acetic acid and 0.1 mL of 7.5 M hydroxylamine-HCl were then added. The samples were mixed with glacial acetic acid to a volume of 10 mL. After that, glacial acetic acid was used to dilute the samples 100 times in order to create the sample extraction solution. The sample extraction solution's absorbance was measured at 280 nm.

It was determined how many bacteria, mold, and yeast there were overall in the CFC. After mixing a 10 g CFC sample with 100 mL of sterile water and agitating it for 20 min, sterile water was serially diluted. Freshly prepared medium was used to cultivate the cells. The entire population of bacteria was grown on nutrient agar medium for a full day at 37 °C. Mold and yeast were cultured on Potato Dextrose Agar medium for 48 h at 28 °C.

Every experimental outcome is reported as mean and standard deviation and is acquired in at least three duplicates. Duncan's multiple range test for mean separation and Tukey-Kramer tests when there were significant differences (p < 0.05) were conducted using the IBM SPSS Statistics 26 program. The data analysis was also managed with Excel 2019 and Origin 2022.

The size distribution of the CDs evaluated by dynamic light-scattering was 0.54–1.11 nm, as indicated in Fig. 1A. TEM imaging was used to observe the morphology of the CDs, as Fig. 1B illustrates. The created CDs had a spherical shape that was monodispersed. As a result, CDs made from banana peel were smaller than the standard CDs size range of 10 nm (Rui et al., 2023). According to Fan et al. (Fan, Zhang, Fan, & Jiang, 2019), CDs from kelp showed a consistent quasispherical shape with a size distribution of 0.54–0.83 nm. In addition, the sample's zeta potential was measured in order to ascertain its surface charge. High zeta potential particle surface charges have a tendency to resist one another, stabilize the dispersion, and preserve functionality. Absolute values that are far from zero indicate increased solution stability. The manufactured CDs had a zeta potential of about 11.9 mV. This implied that the charged functional groups' electrostatic repulsion might aid in stabilizing the CDs (Wu et al., 2024).

The ultraviolet section of the UV–visible spectra of CDs (Fig. 1D) often shows absorption peaks at 233 nm and 279 nm. This peak is commonly associated with the π-π* transition of the CC bond and the n-π* transition of the CO bond, as explained by Fu et al. (Fu et al., 2022). Due to the presence of these peaks, the CDs possess the capacity to assimilate UV radiation. Utilizing this capacity on the packaging film would successfully protect food from the harmful effects of ambient UV radiation, preventing deterioration and decay. In daylight, the CDs solution had a yellowish appearance, but when it was subjected to a 365 nm UV lamp, blue fluorescence was seen (see the picture in Fig. 1D). The influence of excitation wavelength on the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs was shown in Fig. 1E, the emission intensity shows excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior and is reliant on the excitation wavelength (Wu et al., 2024).

Using Raman scattering, the chemical properties of CDs were examined; the findings are shown in Fig. 1F. The D (structural flaws) and G (graphitic order) bands are represented by the two distinctive peaks, which are located at 1366 and 1568 cm−1, respectively (Hoang, Nguyen, & Gomes, 2019). The D to G peak intensity ratio is used to assess the materials' structural disorder. This confirmed surface/edge defects due to the functional groups of biomass-derived CDs. Fig. 1G displays the XRD pattern of CDs, which exhibits a wide diffraction peak at 2θ values of 20.8°, corresponding to (113) diffraction of graphitic frameworks. This peak corresponds to the lattice spacing of graphitic carbon, indicating that the structure of the CDs closely resembles that of graphite. The functional groups of CDs were subsequently identified using FTIR (Fig. 1H). The presence of OH stretching vibration groups was identified using FTIR at 3401 cm−1 (Zhao et al., 2020). The peaks seen at 2942 cm−1 correspond to distinct CH stretching vibrations, as identified by Barman et al. (Barman, Handegård, Hashimoto, & Nagao, 2021). The stretching and bending vibrations of the CO and CC bonds corresponded to the stretching vibrations at 1631 cm−1 and 1592 cm−1 (Mei et al., 2022), respectively. In addition, the O=C − OH stretching vibration and C − O stretching vibration display peaks at 1404 cm−1 and 1049 cm−1 (Meng et al., 2019), respectively. Thus, the Raman, XRD and FTIR spectra confirm the successful formation of CDs from banana peel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Complexation between CDs and SC: fluorescence quenching kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: Proteins' primary fluorescent groups are hydrophobic amino acid residues (Tyr and Trp), which endows proteins with natural fluorescence. The intensity of the fluorescence decreases when the protein and quenching agents are mixed together in a solution (Liu et al., 2023). Consequently, protein endogenous fluorescence quenching can be used to study the interaction between proteins and hydrophobic bioactive compounds. Fig. 2 shows the typical intrinsic fluorescence emission profiles of sericin, in the presence of increasing concentrations of CDs (0–1.0 mg/mL). The maximum fluorescence intensity gradually dropped with increasing CDs concentration, indicating that complexation with CDs has quenched the inherent fluorescence of sericin. Sericin's rose from 336 to 340 nm, with a red-shift (3–4 nm) of λm seen in this case. The red-shift shows how sericin's fluorescence chromophore (Trp) is changing in the direction of a more polar microenvironment (Liu, Li, Zhang, & Tang, 2019). It's due to the complexation of sericin with CDs. Similar phenomena have been well observed for the complexation of proteins with Thymol (Liu et al., 2024), the interactions between Lepidium sativum protein hydrolysate and curcumin (Kadam, Palamthodi, & Lele, 2019), or between whey protein and theaflavin (Xu, Huang, Wei, Weng, & Wei, 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of the nanocomposite particles
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3A summarizes the particle size distribution of the nanocomposite particles of CQAS, SC, and CQSC with apparent diagram of CQSC. With a typical Z-average diameter of 58.8 nm, the majority of the particles in CQSC solutions were nanoparticles. The complexation of CQAS and SC with CDs produced a notably higher particle size, as expected. And CQSC became denser and more uniform compared with CQAS and SC (Wu et al., 2024). Combined with TEM micrographs (Fig. 3B, C), it could be seen clearly that after introduction of CDs into CQS nanocomposite particles, the resultant CQSC composites remains the nano size and the CDs is not aggregated. The obtained CQSC nanocomposite particles have an average size of ∼58.8 nm, which is similar to previously reported CDs nanocomposite particles (Khatoon, Iqbal, & Ahmad, 2016).

Fig. 3D depicted the XRD spectrum of samples, including CDs, SC, CQAS, and CQSC. The SC powder displayed distinct peaks in its diffraction pattern at 2θ of 19.75°, indicating its β-sheet structure and belonging to the crystalline phase of SC (Khanapure, Sherapura, & Vootla, 2023; Mei et al., 2022). CQAS had a wide diffraction peak near 2θ = 19.9°, and the crystallinity was 52.57 % (Lin, Song, Bin, Liao, & Jiang, 2011). The CQAS crystal area suffered damage due to the influence of SC and CDs. The presence of SC and CDs hindered CQAS's ability to develop a new crystalline structure and caused it to take on an amorphous form (CQSC). Tian's study (Tian et al., 2023) showed that the incorporation of peppermint essential oil (PMO) changed the semi-crystalline state of the soy protein isolate-chitosan quaternary ammonium salt complex solution to an amorphous form. Because CDs' molecule was smaller than PMO's and easier to embed in SC's hydrophobic cavity, it restricted the movement of SC and CQAS molecules, which in turn altered the crystal structure.

FT-IR analysis was used to determine the distinctive peaks of the nanocomposite particles (Fig. 3E). The OH and NH stretching vibrations for the SC powder are shown by the large peaks at 3150–3550 cm−1 (Min et al., 2020). The amide I, amide II, and amide III peaks are located at 1635 cm−1, 1528 cm−1, and 1397 cm−1, respectively (Wang, Tang, Huang, & Hui, 2020). The CH bond of the methyl groups and the NH bond in the main amine were responsible for the peaks in the CQAS spectra at 1479 cm−1 and 1652 cm−1, respectively (Min et al., 2020). The OH and NH bonds between hydroxyl and amine groups were identified as the source of the distinctive peak at 3293 cm−1 (Zhang, Tan, Li, Liu, & Guo, 2021). For the CQSC, the characteristic peaks at 1652 cm−1 and 1397 cm−1 were observed. The research above indicates that the hydrogen connection between the CQAS and SC allows for interaction. The spectra also show that there have been changes to the CQSC's peak sizes and locations. The aforementioned findings all point to the possibility that blending may encourage the development of intra- or intermolecular hydrogen bond networks between constituents.

The Oxford-cup technique was utilized to evaluate the bacteriostatic characteristics of CQSC nanocomposite particles against two prevalent pathogenic bacteria, namely E. coli (Gram-negative bacteria) and B. subtilis (Gram-positive bacteria). The nanocomposite particles exhibited inhibitory circles at four dosages of 0, 0.2, 0.6, and 1.0 mg/mL in Fig. 4 , it was evident that the concentration of CDs significantly enhanced the antimicrobial activity of the composite coatings. Table 1 illustrates how the concentration of CDs enhanced the inhibition zone widths against E. coli and B. subtilis, which increased from 14.20 mm to 49.04 mm and from 12.31 mm to 35.82 mm, respectively. In contrast, pure CQS exhibited slight detectable inhibitory activity. The characteristics of the active filler CDs are responsible for the coatings' enhanced antibacterial activity (Wu et al., 2024). This might be the result of the holes in bacterial films being nanoscale, but bacteria themselves are microscale creatures. Furthermore, the compact size and large specific surface area of CDs enable them to live in a dynamic manner in the biofilm, where they penetrate the bacterial cell wall and release intracellular components that ultimately lead to the death of the bacteria (Varghese & Balachandran, 2021). Moreover, compared to B. subtilis, E. coli responds better to the CQSC nanocomposite particles in terms of therapeutic efficacy. This difference in the bacterial cell outer membrane's structure may be the cause of this. Comparable behavioral patterns were seen in the carbon dots, chitosan, and silk sericin films (Mei et al., 2022). Bacterial contamination may be avoided when the composite layer is applied during fruits and vegetables preservation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microbial load and quality of fresh-cut Chinese flowering cabbage

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows the changes in the appearance of Chinese flowering cabbage (CFC) during storage. Fresh CFC has the best possible appearance quality on the day of harvest, with characteristics like smoothness, straightness, luster, etc. With the prolongation of storage time, the leaves of fresh CFC gradually turned yellow and wilt due to water loss. It is evident that the CQS coating slowed down the rate of deterioration, and that the coating enhanced by CDs performed better. The samples with CQSC-1.0 kept the best look, whereas the control group's CFC leaves showed the most withering and yellowing. As a matter of fact, CFC causes a variety of physiological processes that lead to the destruction of chlorophyll during storage, which is the primary reason of the yellowing of CFC leaves (Wang et al., 2024). As the storage duration extended, spinach treated with CQSC-1.0 showed the least color change, as seen by Figs. 5 and 6C-Color. This might possibly be connected to spinach's chlorophyll deterioration.

Fruit and vegetable weight loss is a reflection of water lost during storage (Zhang, Zhang, Devahastin, & Guo, 2019). All samples had a progressive rise in weight loss over the course of the 8 d storage period (Fig. 6A). The weight loss of CFC during storage was considerably postponed by all coating treatments; however, CQSC-1.0 treatment proved to be the most effective. The weight loss of CFC with CQSC-1.0 was only 2.52 % throughout the course of the storage period, a substantial decrease from the control group's result of 12.64 %. Furthermore, wilting and texture loss may be directly related to water loss. The weight loss of samples with CQSC after 8 d (3.80 %) was lower than that of samples with CQS and control samples after 2 d (4.21 % and 5.51 %), which was also reflected by the appearance in Fig. 6C-Texture. These findings suggested that coatings, particularly CQSC, might function as water barriers and effectively prevent water loss from CFC surfaces in order to preserve the CFC's appearance quality. Similar phenomena were observed in strawberries packaged in a composite film fabricated by quaternary ammonium salt modified chitosan (HACC) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (Min et al., 2020).

Chlorophyll content is an effective indicator for determining the pace at which CFCs are yellowing since it can represent how green vegetables change color during storage and preservation (Chen & Fan, 2021). In every instance, the amount of chlorophyll showed a declining trend (Fig. 6B), which was also reflected by the appearance in Fig. 6C-Color; however, the sample that was exposed to CQSC had a considerably larger amount of chlorophyll than the control. The chlorophyll content of CQSC-1.0 was 260.41 g/kg after 8 days of storage, compared to 150.88 g/kg for the control sample and 194.82 g/kg for the sample that underwent CQS. The findings presented show that CDs 1 is the ideal treatment condition and that coatings containing CDs may aid in the retention of chlorophylls.

As shown in Fig. 6C, the sensory evaluation of CFC treated in various solutions was evaluated during storage. It is clear from integrating the data from Figs. 5 and 6C that during the first four days of storage, there were no appreciable changes to the sensory or appearance of CFC. Both the high quality and distinctive scent of CFC were retained. But after 6-day, there were observable variations in the groups' sensory characteristics. CFC that had been treated with pure water and CQS coating became limp and yellow in appearance, making it unfit for consumption. On the 8-day, there was a localized browning of CFC along with a reduction in their aroma. On the other hand, during the storage time, CFC that had been treated with CQSC-0.2 and − 1.0 showed varied degrees of improved freshness. This implies that the shelf life of fresh CFC was positively extended by the inclusion of CDs to the CQS solution. These CFC kept their distinctive flavor and texture, suggesting that CQSC successfully postponed the deterioration and spoiling of CFC, extending their shelf life. Similar phenomena were observed in CDs with chitosan coating treated modified-atmosphere-packaged fresh-cut cucumber (Fan et al., 2019).

The lignification of the stem, which results in the development of hardened and hollowed stems and changes the texture and mouthfeel, is one of the main features of senescence in CFC (Wang, Li, & Fan, 2022). According to Fig. 7A, all treatments' lignin contents progressively rose over time in storage. The four treatment samples' lignin content was significantly decreased after eight days when compared to the control. By the conclusion of storage, the lignin content of the control had increased the most, from 445.98 mg/100 g to 1126.40 mg/100 g. Following eight days of storage, the sample treated with CQSC-1.0 had the lowest lignin concentration (760.95 mg/100 g), followed by those treated with CQSC-0.2 (788.49 mg/100 g) and CQS (1033.66 mg/100 g). According to the findings, CQSC were able to inhibit lignin production in CFC more successfully than CQS coating on its own.

Longer storage times resulted in a general reduction in the ascorbic acid concentration of CFC in both the CT and experimental groups, as Fig. 7B illustrates. The reasons for this reduction include microbial invasion, self-respiration, and external oxidation during storage (Wang, Lu, Xu, Hu, & Liu, 2019). The ascorbic acid concentrations of the CQS and CQSC groups were about 25 mg/100 g on the 8-day of storage, but the CT group's contents were only roughly 12 mg/100 g. It is evident that the CQSC successfully reduced the ascorbic acid churn rate. It is evident that the ascorbic acid retention rate was successfully decreased by the CQS and CQSC. This is explained by the CQS coating's capacity to partially block oxygen, which stops ascorbic acid in CFC from degrading. Furthermore, the ascorbic acid consumption was decreased more by the CQS coatings containing CDs than by the CQS coating alone. This might be as a result of the former's antimicrobial qualities in addition to its ability to separate oxygen and prevent respiration. Our results are in line with another study where the use of a microemulsion of cinnamon essential oil substantially stopped the loss of ascorbic acid and avoided oxidative damage (Chen, Hu, & Zhang, 2024).

The data on total colony and the mold and yeast count on CQSC coated Chinese flowering cabbage (CFC) during storage are shown in Figs. 7C, D. All samples exhibited an increase in the overall number of colonies as well as mold and yeast with longer storage times. The total number of colonies after 8 days of storage was 9.42, 6.77, 4.94, and 4.05 log CFU/g for the control, CQS, CQSC-0.2, and − 1.0. For the control, CQS, CQSC-0.2, and CQSC-1.0, the mold and yeast counts were 9.22, 8.77, 5.54, and 4.92 log CFU/g, respectively. Some western nations have developed microbiological safety guidelines now in effect for fresh-cut fruits and vegetables whose total colony counts are 105 or 106 CFU/g. These results show that when compared to the control, the CQSC coated samples had a much lower overall colony count as well as a lower count of mold and yeast. Notably, the CQSC-1.0 outperformed the other coatings and showed a favorable bacteriostatic effect on CFC. This is explained by the addition of CDs containing plenty of functional groups to the CQSC, which increased the CQSC's antibacterial activity against mold, yeast, and total colony count. These findings support the antibacterial action shown in Fig. 4 of the CQSC, highlighting its potent suppression of microbial growth. It has been verified using carbon dots that bio-based coatings applied to food surfaces prevent enzymatic browning and moisture dissipation, regulate gas exchange, and provide a semi-permeable barrier that further prevents microbial-induced spoiling (Cui et al., 2022). The findings from previous studies state that bacteria (Pseudomonas aeruginosa)-derived carbon dots (PCDs) can reduce microbial contamination of pork meat (Hong, Riahi, Shin, & Kim, 2024). Similar results were also presented by Zhao (Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, Adhikari, & Wang, 2022), who found that coated with carbon dots (CDs) loaded polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) active food packaging films can significantly reduce microbial number of banana, jujube, and fried meatballs during storage.

In the current study, CDs were successfully prepared from biomass through a straightforward one-step hydrothermal process. These CDs have strong biocompatibility, anti-oxidative properties, bacteriostasis, and easy substrate contact. The CQAS and SC were combined with CDs to create a bio-nanocomposite coating that possessed antioxidant, biocompatibility, and bacteriostasis properties. The effects of different concentrations of CDs on the physicochemical, structural, and functional activity of nanocomposite particles were tested. The results in XRD and FT-IR show that CDs were extraordinarily compounded with CQAS and SC. Protein quenching caused by endogenous fluorescence was lessened by the interaction of SC and CDs. And the nanocomposite particles had a certain degree inhibitory impact on E. coli and B. subtilis. The CQSC composite coating treatment also decreased mass losses, retarded the increase of lignin content, preserved the ascorbic acid and chlorophyll levels in CFC, inhibited microbial development, and preserved the distinctive texture and aroma of CFC when applied to storage and preservation. However, because of changes in sensory qualities, the shelf life was limited to 8 days. In conclusion, the bio-based composite coating from CQSC is a stable and successful active food preservation packaging material that has a large scale to replace petroleum-based packaging.

Zhaojun Ban: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Linxuan Jin: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yueyue Zhang: Formal analysis. Haobin Chen: Visualization, Investigation. Houxue Li: Resources. Feiping Chen: Software. Lingling Liu: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Mehdi Abddollahi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
